author: @CBaller2020
text:
This movie and the outrage makes me cry. I have a 22-year-old son who's autistic and developmentally delayed. He has outbursts, can be very loud, and has no filter at times. He's had a ton of encounters from a very young age with people telling him to shut the F up. Adults telling a young child to shut up, die, go k!ll yourself, and being physically and mentally abused by his 2 teachers. 
I don't expect people to be nice all the time, but when I tell people what his conditions are, I expect a tiny bit of understanding and empathy. 
The fact that people are trying to cancel this man while trying to bring awareness and understanding shows me how people are truly blind.

author: @lacountess
text: This movie hits me hard. My advanced ADHD causes a similar condition during high stress situations. Years ago, I accidentally called a United Airlines gate agent a B)tch. I didn't even realize I said it until it was too late and all I could do was apologize over and over. She didn't seem to mind but her white knighting supervisor butted in and not only banned me and my family from boarding our connection flight, he called the airport security to "deal with me properly." That was the first time I heard about Tourette's because he mocked me for naming the wrong condition. Thankfully one of the security guards was kind enough to lead us to another airline and explain to us what to do now that our bags were gone with the connection flight. I'll never forget that small kindness in a time when everything seemed to crash on me.

​@TheWorldisRaw Not everyone with Tourettes has a tic that involves saying inappropriate things. I had two friends in college with the condition - one had only the physical tics, the other had physical tics and a mild speech tic that didn't involve swearing. Tics tended to get worse when he was tired or overwhelmed, but years of practice and taking OCD-related medication helped minimize the disability. 

The man in the movie I Swear seems to have a really severe condition. It really is involuntary.

author: @MassMark1965
text:
I spent the last 30-years of my life working with people who have developmental disabilities - from Downs Syndrome, to Autism and Tourette’s. I cannot wait to see this movie. 

Several years ago, my staff and I took our ‘crew’ of 23 people out for a picnic. We were talking over coffee, as our people relaxed in the sunshine. One of my staff said: “This should be a movie”. There was a lot of wisdom in that statement, as so many of the people we serve, go largely unnoticed by society. 

It’s heartwarming to me, that I Swear, gives so many a voice - a story, the attention so long overdue...

replies.author: @MassMark1965

author: @stanleydangerfreak2325
text:
I remember seeing the original documentary about him when I was a boy, 'John's Not Mad' it was called, because some people thought he must be insane. Nobody had heard of Tourette syndrome at that time, we knew nothing. I remember finding some of his outbursts funny, but it was obvious to my whole family as we watched the documentary unfold that this was awful for him and his family.
I've just watched the movie this evening and it is those humble moments of compassion, the simple triumphs that we might take for granted that are the most moving. John's story made me feel glad for the simple triumphs in life. The scene when he is able to enter a library for the first time since he was 14 without causing a scene was surprisingly moving.

I used to watch a lot of Beyond the Trailer, but just stopped because most videos had some point that I felt was just way off the mark. One of the main things she had “odd” points on was in regards to queer representation. 

For example, if there was a queer character that wasn’t at all flamboyant, she would say they’re portrayed as “too straight”. 

Or if a bi character ever dated someone of the opposite gender, then they’re “straight”. Even if that bi character was in a TV show where they previously dated someone of the same gender. 

Or a gay character played by a straight actor, she would say that’s discrimination. History here, gay characters were allowed in media before gay actors or writers etc. So to force media to do the bare minimum, they said if they wanted to use queer characters, they had to use queer actors too. But nowadays, since gay people generally can get acting work, that stipulation is not required. If it continued at this point, it would just limit the roles people can have. She clearly has heard of these issues, but doesn’t actually understand them enough to know why it was wrong in the past but ok now. 

She kinda feels like what an extremely right wing person thinks a “woke” person is.

I truly hope this movie makes a difference in the social dynamic surrounding this affliction.

text: My fraternity brother in college had tourettes, not verbal but he had tics. They were loud enough tics to be audible but it was so eye-opening to have someone like that as a friend, he helped explain how it was and how it affected him. I still remember sitting down and chatting and he said "imagine it's like a really bad cough when you are sick, you can hold it back but not stop it and it feels worse if you try". He's a good man, his wife is epileptic so they were a funny pair that always made light of their difficulties.
